subject: Physics
q: A radioactive sample has an average life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></math> and is decaying. A capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is first charged and later connected with resistor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the ratio of the charge on the capacitor to the activity of the radioactive sample is fixed with respect to time then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> should be<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_______Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A radioactive sample has an average life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></math> and is decaying. A capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is first charged and later connected with resistor <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If the ratio of the charge on the capacitor to the activity of the radioactive sample is fixed with respect to time then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> should be<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_______Ω</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Tm=30 ms
C=200 μF
qN=Q0e-tRCN0e-λ,t=Q0 N0eλ-1RC

Since qN is constant hence
λ=1RC
R=1λC=TmC=30×10-3200×10-6=150 Ω

q: Two radioactive elements <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> initially have same number of atoms. The half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is same as the average life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are decay constants of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> respectively, then choose the correct relation from the given options.
question: Two radioactive elements <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> initially have same number of atoms. The half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is same as the average life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>A</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> are decay constants of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> respectively, then choose the correct relation from the given options.
solution:
It is given that 

T12(A)=Tav(B)

The half life of a substance is given by T12=ln2λ

So,

ln2λA=1λB

⇒λA=λBln2

subject: Physics
q: Nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is having mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn></math> and its binding energy per nucleon is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>. It splits in two fragments <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> of mass numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>105</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>115</mn></math>. The binding energy of nucleons in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> per nucleon. The energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> released per fission will be:
question: Nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is having mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn></math> and its binding energy per nucleon is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math>. It splits in two fragments <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> of mass numbers <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>105</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>115</mn></math>. The binding energy of nucleons in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> per nucleon. The energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> released per fission will be:
solution:
The total binding energy of nucleus A is,

EA=220×5.6=1232 MeV

The total binding energy of B and C combined is,

EB+EC=105×6.4+115×6.4=1408 MeV

Therefore, the energy released per fission is,

Q=EB+EC-EA=1408-1232=176 MeV

subject: Physics
q:
The energy released per fission of nucleus of X240 is 200 MeV. The energy released if all the atoms in 120 g of pure X240 undergo fission is _____×1025 MeV.  

(Given NA=6×1023)
question:
The energy released per fission of nucleus of X240 is 200 MeV. The energy released if all the atoms in 120 g of pure X240 undergo fission is _____×1025 MeV.  

(Given NA=6×1023)
solution:
Energy released per fission is 200 MeV.

The number of atoms n in 120 g:

n=120240×6×1023 atoms.

Total energy released, E=n×200×106

=6×1025 MeV

q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : The nuclear density of nuclides B510, L36i, F2656e, N1020e and B83209i can be arranged as ρBiN>ρFeN>ρNeN>ρBN>ρLiN

Reason R : The radius R of nucleus is related to its mass number A as R=R0 A13, where R0 is a constant.

In the light of the above statement, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : The nuclear density of nuclides B510, L36i, F2656e, N1020e and B83209i can be arranged as ρBiN>ρFeN>ρNeN>ρBN>ρLiN

Reason R : The radius R of nucleus is related to its mass number A as R=R0 A13, where R0 is a constant.

In the light of the above statement, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
A is false but R is true
A is true but R is false
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
solution:
Nuclear density is given by, ρ=m43πR03, where m is mass of proton. Clearly it is independent of A and same for all nucleus.

Radius of the nucleus is given by, R=R0 A13.

However, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> disintegrates to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> disintegrates to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math>. Which of the following figures represents the evolution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced></mrow><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></math> with respect to time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math>?
question: At time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, a material is composed of two radioactive atoms <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> The decay constant of both kind of radioactive atoms is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. However, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> disintegrates to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> disintegrates to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math>. Which of the following figures represents the evolution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced></mrow><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></math> with respect to time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math>?
solution:
The rate at which nuclei of B is forming, 

dNBdt=λNA-λNB

dNBdt=2λNB0e-λt-λNB

e-λtdNBdt+λNB=2λNB0e-λteλt

ddtNBe-λt=2λNB0

on integrating above the equation, 
NBeλt=2λtNB+NB0
NBeλt=2λtNB0+NB0

NB=NB1+2λte-λtddtNB=0

-λ1+2λte-λt+2λe-λt=0NBmax at t=12λ

Mass of a nucleon is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and radius of nucleus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The approximate ratio of the nuclear density and water density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>13</mn></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> is _____.
question: Assume that protons and neutrons have equal masses. Mass of a nucleon is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and radius of nucleus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>15</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The approximate ratio of the nuclear density and water density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>13</mn></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> is _____.
solution:
As we know, density of nuclei= mass of nuclei  volume of nuclei 

⇒ρ=1.6×10-27A43π1.5×10-153A

=1.6×10-2714.14×10-45=0.113×1018 kg m-3

We also know that, density of water is ρw=103 kg m-3.

Hence,
ρρw=11.31×1013.

Therefore, n≈11

subject: Physics
q:
The average energy released per fission for the nucleus of ${ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{U}$ is 190 MeV. When all the atoms of 47 g pure ${ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{U}$ undergo fission process, the energy released is $\alpha \times 10^{23} \mathrm{MeV}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
(Avogadro Number $=6 \times 10^{23}$ per mole)
question:
The average energy released per fission for the nucleus of ${ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{U}$ is 190 MeV. When all the atoms of 47 g pure ${ }_{92}^{235} \mathrm{U}$ undergo fission process, the energy released is $\alpha \times 10^{23} \mathrm{MeV}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
(Avogadro Number $=6 \times 10^{23}$ per mole)
solution:
The number of $^{235}\text{U}$ atoms in 47 g is determined using Avogadro's number.
Molar mass of $^{235}\text{U}$ is 235 g/mol, so the number of moles is $n = 47/235$ mol.
The total number of atoms is $N = (47/235) \times 6 \times 10^{23}$.
Since each fission releases 190 MeV, the total energy released is:
$E_{total} = N \times 190 = \frac{47 \times 6 \times 10^{23}}{235} \times 190 = \frac{47 \times 6 \times 190}{235} \times 10^{23}$ MeV.
Calculating the numerator: $47 \times 6 \times 190 = 282 \times 190 = 53580$.
Therefore: $E_{total} = \frac{53580}{235} \times 10^{23} = 228 \times 10^{23}$ MeV, giving $\alpha = 228$.

subject: Physics
q: What is the half-life period of a radioactive material if its activity drops to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msup><mn>16</mn><mrow><mi>th</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></mfrac></math> of its initial value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> years ?
question: What is the half-life period of a radioactive material if its activity drops to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msup><mn>16</mn><mrow><mi>th</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></mfrac></math> of its initial value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> years ?
solution:
Activity of a radioactive material is given by A=A0e-λt, here, λ- is decay constant.

Taking logarithmic function both sides, 

⇒-λt=lnAA0

Using relation between half life and decay constant,

⇒-ln2t12×30=ln116 

 ⇒-ln2t12×30=-4ln2

⇒t12=304=7.5 yrs

q: Which of the following radiations has the least wavelength?
question: Which of the following radiations has the least wavelength?
solution:
$\mathrm{R} \rightarrow$ Radio waves ; $\mathrm{M} \rightarrow$ Micro waves; $\quad \mathrm{I} \rightarrow$ Infra red rays; $\mathrm{V} \rightarrow$ Visible rays; $\mathrm{U} \rightarrow$ Ultraviolet rays; $\mathrm{X} \rightarrow \mathrm{X}$ rays; $\quad \mathrm{G} \rightarrow \gamma$ rays; $\quad \mathrm{C} \rightarrow$ Cosmic rays
$\Rightarrow \gamma$ rays has least wavelength

subject: Physics
q: Two radioactive substances <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> have decay constants <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> respectively. At<math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, a sample has the same number of the two nuclei. The time taken for the ratio of the number of nuclei to become <math><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be
question: Two radioactive substances <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> have decay constants <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">λ</mi></math> respectively. At<math><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, a sample has the same number of the two nuclei. The time taken for the ratio of the number of nuclei to become <math><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> will be
solution:
Using the decay equation, N=N0e-λt
For radioactive substance A, N1=N0e-5λt ...........(i)
For radioactive substance B, N2=N0e-λt ............(ii)
From equation (i) and (ii)
N1N2=e-5λte-λt
1e2=e-4λt
4λt=2
Time taken, t=12λ

subject: Physics
q: Two nuclei of mass number $3$ combine with another nucleus of mass number $4$ to yield a nucleus of mass number $10$. If the binding energy per nucleon for the mass numbers $3$, $4$ and $10$ are $5.6$ MeV, $7.4$ MeV and $6.1$ MeV, respectively, then in the process, $\Delta Mc^2 = $ _____ MeV.
question: Two nuclei of mass number $3$ combine with another nucleus of mass number $4$ to yield a nucleus of mass number $10$. If the binding energy per nucleon for the mass numbers $3$, $4$ and $10$ are $5.6$ MeV, $7.4$ MeV and $6.1$ MeV, respectively, then in the process, $\Delta Mc^2 = $ _____ MeV.
solution:
The nuclear reaction is given by:

$2 X^{3} + Y^{4} \rightarrow Z^{10}$

The total binding energy of the reactants is the sum of the binding energies of the two nuclei of mass number $3$ and the nucleus of mass number $4$.

Binding energy of reactants $= 2 \times (3 \times 5.6) + (4 \times 7.4)$

$= 33.6 + 29.6 = 63.2$ MeV

The total binding energy of the product nucleus of mass number $10$ is:

Binding energy of product $= 10 \times 6.1 = 61.0$ MeV

The change in mass energy $\Delta M c^2$ is equal to the difference in the total binding energies of the reactants and the product.

$\Delta M c^2 = |63.2 - 61.0| = 2.2$ MeV

Answer: $2.2$

q: The mass number of nucleus having radius equal to half of the radius of nucleus with mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>192</mn></math> is:

Given: R1=R22

As we know, size of the nucleus is given by R=R0A1/3

Therefore, we can write R0A11/3=R02A21/3

⇒A1=18A2

⇒A1=1928=24

subject: Physics
q: For a nucleus of mass number $A$ and radius $R$, the mass density of nucleus can be represented as
question: For a nucleus of mass number $A$ and radius $R$, the mass density of nucleus can be represented as
solution:
The mass density ( $\rho$) of a nucleus is defined as the ratio of its mass to its volume:
$\rho=$ Mass of nucleus / Volume of nucleus
For a spherical nucleus with radius R , the volume is:
$\text { Volume }=(4 / 3) \pi R^3$
The mass of the nucleus is related to the mass number A :
$\text { Mass }-\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{m} \text { _u }$
(where m_u is the atomic mass unit, approximately $1.66 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$)
Therefore:
$\rho=\left(\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{m} \_\mathrm{u}\right) /\left[(4 / 3) \pi \mathrm{R}^3\right]$
Now, there's an important relationship between the mass number A and nuclear radius R. Empirically, it has been found that:
$\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{Ro} * \mathrm{~A}^{\wedge}(1 / 3)$
Where Ro is a constant approximately equal to $1.2 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{~m}(1.2$ fermi).
Substituting this into the density equation:
$\begin{aligned}
& \rho=\left(\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{m} \_\mathrm{u}\right) /\left[(4 / 3) \pi\left(\mathrm{Ro} \times \mathrm{A}^{\wedge}(1 / 3)\right)^3\right] \\ & \rho=(\mathrm{A} \times \mathrm{m} \text { _u }) /\left[(4 / 3) \pi \mathrm{Ro}^3 \times \mathrm{A}\right] \\ & \rho=\mathrm{m} \_\mathrm{u} /\left[(4 / 3) \pi \mathrm{Ro}^3\right]
\end{aligned}$
This shows that the nuclear density is approximately constant for all nuclei, regardless of their mass number. This is a fundamental property of nuclear matter known as nuclear saturation density.
The numerical value is:
$\rho=2.3 \times 10^{17} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3$

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following Statements is correct?
question: Which of the following Statements is correct?
options: The rate of radioactive decay cannot be controlled but that of nuclear fission can be controlled.
Nuclear forces are short range, attractive and charge dependent.
Nuclei of atoms having same number of neutrons are known as isobars.
Wavelength of matter waves is given by de Broglie formula but that of photons is not given by the same formula
solution: Radioactive decay is a continuous process. Rate of radioactive decay cannot be controlled. Nuclear fission can be controlled but not of nuclear fusion.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : The binding energy per nucleon is practically independent of the atomic number for nuclei of mass number in the range 30 to 170.

Reason R : Nuclear force is short ranged.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A : The binding energy per nucleon is practically independent of the atomic number for nuclei of mass number in the range 30 to 170.

Reason R : Nuclear force is short ranged.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
solution:
The binding energy for stable nuclei is always a positive number, as the nucleus must gain energy for the nucleons to move apart from each other. Nucleons are attracted to each other by the strong nuclear force. Binding energy per nucleon is almost constant in the range of 30-170. This is because nuclear energy is a short range force. It operates in the femto metre region.

Hence, this option is the correct one.

q: The activity of a radioactive material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>56</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Ci</mi></math>. If the half life of the material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> days, after how many days the activity will become <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Ci</mi></math>?
question: The activity of a radioactive material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>56</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Ci</mi></math>. If the half life of the material is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> days, after how many days the activity will become <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Ci</mi></math>?
solution:
The activity of a radioactive sample at any time t is given by,

A=A02-tT12⇒2×10-5=2.56×10-3×2-t5⇒2t5=128⇒t5=7⇒t=35 days

subject: Physics
q: The energy released if hydrogen atoms are combined to form $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ is __________ MeV. (Take binding energies per nucleon of $^{2}_{1}\text{H}$ and $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ as $1.1$ MeV and $7.2$ MeV, respectively)
question: The energy released if hydrogen atoms are combined to form $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ is __________ MeV. (Take binding energies per nucleon of $^{2}_{1}\text{H}$ and $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ as $1.1$ MeV and $7.2$ MeV, respectively)
solution:
The fusion reaction is given by:

$2 ^{2}_{1}\text{H} \rightarrow ^{4}_{2}\text{He}$

Binding energy of one $^{2}_{1}\text{H}$ nucleus = $2 \times 1.1 = 2.2$ MeV

Total initial binding energy = $2 \times 2.2 = 4.4$ MeV

Binding energy of $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ nucleus = $4 \times 7.2 = 28.8$ MeV

Energy released $\Delta E$ = Final binding energy - Initial binding energy

$\Delta E = 28.8 - 4.4 = 24.4$ MeV

Answer: $24.4$

q: In a radioactive decay chain, the initial nucleus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi> </mi><mn>90</mn><mn>232</mn></msubsup><mi>Th</mi></math>. At the end, there are <math><mn>6</mn></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particles and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>β</mi></math>-particles which are emitted. If the end nucleus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mrow/><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>Z</mi></math> are given by:
question: In a radioactive decay chain, the initial nucleus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi> </mi><mn>90</mn><mn>232</mn></msubsup><mi>Th</mi></math>. At the end, there are <math><mn>6</mn></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particles and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>β</mi></math>-particles which are emitted. If the end nucleus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mrow/><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msubsup><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>Z</mi></math> are given by:
solution:
As we know that in alpha decay mass number reduced from for and the atomic number reduced by two

and in beta decay the mass number became unchange and atomic number increase from one

Change in Z=6×2-4=8
Change in A=6×4-4=8
TH90232→6α4β PB82208

q: The decay constant for a radioactive nuclide is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Atomic weight of the substance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mole</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup></math>. The activity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of the substance is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Bq</mi></math>.
question: The decay constant for a radioactive nuclide is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Atomic weight of the substance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mole</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup></math>. The activity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>µ</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of the substance is ________ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>10</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Bq</mi></math>.
solution:
It is given that 

λ=1.5×10-5 s-1

Number of moles is 1×10-660=10-76

Number of atoms=number of moles ×NA

N0=10-76×6×1023=1016

Hence, at t=0, A=A0=N0λ, the activity can be given by

A=N0λe-λt⇒A=1016×1.5×10-5×1=15×1010 Bq

subject: Physics
q: The half-life period of a radio-active element $\mathrm{X}$ is same as the mean life time of another radioactive element $\mathrm{Y}$. Initially they have the same number of atoms. Then
question: The half-life period of a radio-active element $\mathrm{X}$ is same as the mean life time of another radioactive element $\mathrm{Y}$. Initially they have the same number of atoms. Then
options: $\mathrm{X}$ and $\mathrm{Y}$ have same decay rate initially
$\mathrm{X}$ and $\mathrm{Y}$ decay at same rate always.
solution:
$\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=\frac{\ln 2}{\lambda_{\mathrm{x}}}$
$\tau_{\text {mean }}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{y}}} ; \frac{\mathrm{dN}}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\lambda \mathrm{N}$
$\frac{\ln 2}{\lambda_{\mathrm{x}}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{y}}} \Rightarrow \lambda_{\mathrm{x}}=\lambda_{\mathrm{y}}(0.6932) \Rightarrow \lambda_{\mathrm{y}}>\lambda_{\mathrm{x}}$

subject: Physics
q: The half-life of a radioactive element $\mathrm{A}$ is the same as the mean-life of another radioactive element B. Initially both substances have the same number of atoms, then :
question: The half-life of a radioactive element $\mathrm{A}$ is the same as the mean-life of another radioactive element B. Initially both substances have the same number of atoms, then :
options: A and B decay at the same rate always.
A and $\mathrm{B}$ decay at the same rate initially.
A will decay at a faster rate than B.
$\mathrm{B}$ will decay at a faster rate than $\mathrm{A}$.
solution:
$\left(\mathrm{T}_{1 / 2}\right)_{\mathrm{A}}=\left(\mathrm{t}_{\text {mean }}\right)_{\mathrm{B}}$
$$
\Rightarrow \frac{0.693}{\lambda_{\mathrm{A}}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{B}}} \Rightarrow \lambda_{\mathrm{A}}=0.693 \lambda_{\mathrm{B}}
$$
or $\lambda_{\mathrm{A}} < \lambda_{\mathrm{B}}$
Also rate of decay $=\lambda \mathrm{N}$
Initially number of atoms $(\mathrm{N})$ of both are equal but since $\lambda_{\mathrm{B}}>\lambda_{\mathrm{A}}$, therefore $\mathrm{B}$ will decay at a faster rate than $\mathrm{A}$

subject: Physics
q:
A nucleus with $\mathrm{Z}=92$ emits the following in a sequence:
$\alpha, \beta^{-}, \beta^{-} \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \beta^{-}, \beta^{-}, \alpha, \beta^{+}, \beta^{+}, \alpha$. Then $\mathrm{Z}$ of the resulting nucleus is
question:
A nucleus with $\mathrm{Z}=92$ emits the following in a sequence:
$\alpha, \beta^{-}, \beta^{-} \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \alpha, \beta^{-}, \beta^{-}, \alpha, \beta^{+}, \beta^{+}, \alpha$. Then $\mathrm{Z}$ of the resulting nucleus is
solution:
No. of $\alpha$ particles emitted $=8, \quad$ No. of $\beta^{-}$particles emitted $=4, \quad$ No.of $\beta^{+}$particles emitted $=2$
$z=92-2 \times 8+4-2=78$

subject: Physics
q: If $M_o$ is the mass of isotope ${ }_5^{12} B, M_P$ and $M_n$ are the masses of proton and neutron, then nuclear binding energy of isotope is :
question: If $M_o$ is the mass of isotope ${ }_5^{12} B, M_P$ and $M_n$ are the masses of proton and neutron, then nuclear binding energy of isotope is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { B.E. }=\Delta m C^2 \\ & \left(5 M_p+7 M_n-M_o\right) C^2\end{aligned}$
